posts.title: Days 1 - 5
posts.content:
In San Diego, I stayed with Scout and Frodo, who host people before they start the PCT. For a couple of months a year, they have 20+ people staying with them. They generously provide breakfast and dinner, give advice, space to prepare packages, and organise shuttles to shops. I am very grateful to have been able to stay here while preparing!My first food stock did not go to plan. Because I am not familiar with the food selection here, I bought way too much. I have sent half of it forward.

I did not anticipate how expensive food would be here. Prices are a lot higher than NZ. In a small general store, I paid $10 USD for a can of chilli beans and instant rice. I’ve learned my lesson there - I’ll stock up more in the bigger towns.

Water is scarce in Southern California, so water carries need to be planned in advance. So far, there’s been an abundance of streams, and I usually only need to carry a couple of liters at a time. Water looks more scarce in the next section, so I will be carrying more soon.

I’ve made it to the town of Julian, which is about 77 miles in. I’m enjoying it so far, and my body seems to he holding up. I have a package waiting ahead, and due to awkward hours at the post office, I’ll be taking the next few days slowly.

posts.title: Days 6 - 12
posts.content:
I’ve made it to Idyllwild, a small town off mile 180 (290 km) of the PCT. The town is next to the San Jacinto Mountains. We rented a large cabin here to share between a bunch of us.

Water has been much more scarce since Julian. A couple of sections are completely dry, and rely on people to bring water in. One of these sources was a water cache, which had several pallets of water bottles. Another was a large tank, which I think is topped up by a truck.

The last couple of days have been quite exhausting. We had lunch at Paradise Valley Cafe (a popular stop on the PCT), and decided to meet in Idyllwild the following evening. This turned out to be quite a big commitment, with about 2500 m elevation gain. Progress was also slowed by blown down trees and detours for water. The trail reached about 2600 m, with sections in the snow. There were some great views towards Palm Springs.

I’m taking a quiet day to recover. Tomorrow, I am considering going to the summit of Mt San Jacinto, which is a short detour from the PCT. The next stop will be in Big Bear.

posts.title: Days 13 - 19
posts.content:
I’m now in Big Bear Lake, which is off mile 275 (443 km) of the PCT. I am just doing a quick resupply here. I arrived this morning, and will head back to the trail this afternoon.

After Idyllwild, I made it to the summit of Mt San Jacinto. We started walking at 4 am and reached the top about an hour after sunrise. There were some amazing views.

I found the next section quite challenging. The trail descends from the mountain, and then ascends back up to Big Bear. The trail follows a river called Mission Creek, which was hit by a massive storm last year. Most of the trail is completely destroyed, meaning a lot of this section passes through a river bed with no track.

Additionally, there has been a recent gastrointestinal illness in this section. The source is unknown, but there is speculation that it is related to the red algae in Mission Creek. There was certainly a lot of it. I carried a lot of water to avoid drinking from here. Luckily, I have avoided the illness (so far).

My next resupply is Wrightwood, which is about 94 miles (151 km) away

posts.title: Day 20 - 26
posts.content:
I’m now in Wrightwood, which is another small mountain town. It’s off mile 369 (594 km) of the PCT - just past the half way point of the desert section. This will be another quick resupply, and I’ll be back on the trail this afternoon.

From Big Bear, the trail followed a river to descend from the mountains. It then crossed the I15 motorway at Cajan Pass. The I15 is quite a busy motorway, connecting Los Angeles and Las Vegas. There was a constant stream of freight trains passing through, which were sometimes kilometers long.

I’m enjoying the variation in the trail. Yesterday, the day started in the desert, and ended in a ski field. Every day is very different.

Unfortunately I’ve had to slow down my pace. I’ve been having trouble with my feet over the last week. I’ve found some exercises and I’ve been given new insoles, which seem to be helping. I hope it’s just a minor setback.

posts.title: Days 27 - 31
posts.content:
I’m now in a town called Agua Dulce, which is at mile 454 (731 km). The PCT passes right through the town, which is great, because I don’t need to worry about hitchhiking. It’s one of the very few road walks I’ve done on the trial.

After Wrightwood, the trail went up Mt Baden-Powell. There were mixed reviews about whether microspikes were needed, with the majority saying the weren’t. I didn’t want to carry them until the next town, so I sent them forward. It ended up being the right decision - there were no problems without them. Like Mt San Jacinto, Baden-Powell had some great views.

For the last 200 or so miles, the trail has been heading west towards the coast - now it starts heading north. Soon, it will pass through the Mojave Desert. This is possibly the driest section, with the longest water carry.

I have a package with new shoes waiting in Tehachapi. I’m hoping to make that my next town stop. It’s about 112 miles away, which will be my biggest distance between resupplies yet. My appetite has grown considerably over the last few weeks, which means I need to carry more food. There are a couple of options along the way if I run out.

I’m still really enjoying the trail. Since I’ve slowed my pace, most of the people I know from the start are well ahead now, but there are always new people to meet. My feet are starting to feel better now, so I’ll start increasing my pace again soon.

posts.title: Days 32 - 37
posts.content:
I’m now in Tehachapi, off mile 559 (899 km) of the PCT. I just had enough food to make it here without another resupply. I will take my first zero day (day without walking any miles on the trail) here, and will finally get some new shoes.

I enjoyed the last few days. The trail went through the Mojave Desert, a particularly hot and dry section with very little shade. It follows the course of the LA aqueduct, which is a key water supply for LA.

Before taking on the aqueduct, a place called Hiker Town is used as a refuge. This is known to be quite an unusual place, with themed cabins that look like they are from a film set. They can be rented for $40, but I chose to stay in my tent after reading reports of bugs inside.

Many people avoid hiking along the aqueduct during peak daylight by leaving in the evening and hiking through the night. This didn’t appeal to me - it would mean waiting around in Hiker Town all of the following day. I decided to get up very early instead. I left just after 3am, which got me past the most exposed part before it got too hot.

After the aqueduct, the trail went through a massive wind farm. The turbines must have been about 100 meters high, and there were hundreds of them. Apparently, it’s the third largest in the world. Unfortunately, wind farms don’t make great camp grounds, and I was kept up most of the night by the wind!

I’m now in the final stretch of the desert section. The next stop is Kenedy Meadows South, which will mark the beginning of the Sierras. This is the section I’m most looking forward to.

posts.title: Days 38 - 46
posts.content:
I’ve made it to Kennedy Meadows, which means I have officially finished the desert section of the PCT. Kennedy meadows is at mile 703 (1,131 km) - just past the quarter way point of the PCT.

It was another challenging section from Tehachapi to Kennedy Meadows. There was a large food carry, multiple large water carries, and lots of elevation gain. This coincided with a heat wave, which made day time hiking unbearable for me. To avoid the heat, I began walking before sunrise every morning - sometimes starting at 3:40 am.

This section of the trail was very dependent on volunteers, who maintain water caches. In one stretch, there were 35 miles between water sources. The water caches reduced this distance to 15 miles. Due to the heat, I needed about 1 litre of water every three miles, so it would be extremely difficult without their help.

I will now move on to the Sierras, which is the high altitude section of the PCT. It can be very challenging depending on the year. Last year was a record snow year, meaning most people had to skip it. Luckily, this year has been an average snow year. Due to the heat wave, the snow had been melting rapidly, so river crossings will probably be the biggest challenge.

I’ve picked up some extra gear. I’ve added microspikes, an ice axe and a bear canister (a requirement to keep food safe from bears). Progress will be slower due to difficult terrain and high altitude, but I am looking forward to the great scenery!

posts.title: Days 47 - 52
posts.subheading: Kennedy Meadows South to Kearsarge Pass
posts.content:
I’m now up to mile 790 (1281 km) of the PCT. I have stopped in a town called Lone Pine to resupply. This resupply took a lot of effort - I took a 7.5 mile side trail over Kearsarge Pass, and two different car rides to get here. I’ll have to do the same in reverse to get back to the trail, with an 800 m climb.

A few days ago, I climbed Mt Whitney, which is a popular detour on the PCT. It has an elevation of 4,421 m, and is the tallest mountain south of Alaska in the USA. It’s apparently extremely difficult to get a permit to climb it from the eastern side, but access is allowed for PCT hikers from the western side.

People usually aim to reach the summit by sunrise. I had missed the sunrise for Mt San Jacinto, so decided to leave extremely early to build in a safety margin. I arrived at the top at about 4:15 am. It was quite cool to see a long line of flashlights as I ascended the mountain.

The following day, we went over Forester Pass. This is the highest point on the PCT, and is at an altitude of just over 4000 m. I seem to have acclimatised ok, but it is quite hard work at this altitude.

My next stop is in a town called Bishop. It’s only 42 trail miles away, but will take at least 3 days due to the large amount of additional off-trail miles I need to cover. Progress is slow, but I don’t mind since the scenery is so nice!

posts.title: Days 53 - 61
posts.content:
I am now in Mammoth Lakes, off mile 908 (1461 km) of the PCT. I will resupply my food and buy new shoes here before heading back to the trail this afternoon. I was lucky to get here on a Friday, as the road to town is closed Monday to Thursday, which would have meant walking an additional 7 mile round trip.

The Sierra has been quite exhausting. Even though we have been walking low miles, I still feel very fatigued by the end of the day. I think it’s mostly due to the high altitude.

Since Lone Pine, the trail has gone over about 6 different passes. The last few have had very limited snow. Since I have already gone over the most difficult ones, I will send my ice axe and microspikes home. It looks like there is less elevation change over the rest of the Sierras, so I should be able to start increasing my mileage. My next town stop will be South Lake Tahoe.

posts.title: Days 62 - 72
posts.subheading: Mammoth Lakes to South Lake Tahoe
posts.content:
I arrived in South Lake Tahoe yesterday, which marks the end of the Sierras. I hitched here from the US50 highway, which is at mile 1092 (1,757 km). I’ll take a day to rest and will head back to the trail tomorrow.

The biggest challenge I faced last week were mosquitos. There were a couple of days where I had a swarm constantly surrounding me. Unfortunately, I ran out of insect repellent, meaning the only solution was to wear my rain gear and head net. This was at a time when the temperature was high, and I had a lot of elevation gain to cover - it was very unpleasant! Fortunately, the mosquitos have not been so bad in the last few days.

Another challenge which I will inevitably face is the threat from wildfires. I have seen smoke from distant fires, and there is already a small trail closure about 500 miles ahead. There’s another fire quite close to the PCT around Lake Tahoe, which may also result in a closure very soon. The region is currently experiencing another heatwave, which is not helping. It seems very unlikely that I’ll finish the trail without having to skip a section due to a fire closure.

Northern California is known to be a mentally challenging section. The great scenery of the Sierras is behind, which will be difficult for motivation. It’s also the stage of the trail where mileage needs to be increased. The gradual start in the desert, and slow pace in the Sierras created a deficit which needs to be made up if I want to finish in time. I’ve worked out the pace I need to be going, and I’ve only just managed to stick to it over the last week. It apparently becomes easier as the trail flattens out, but for now I am finding it quite difficult.

posts.title: Days 73 - 83
posts.content:
I’m now in a town called Chester, at mile 1332 (2144 km). Today, I reached the halfway marker of the PCT, which feels great. The second half should go much faster than the first.

After South Lake Tahoe, the trail went through the Desolation Wilderness. This was a very nice section, with scenery comparable to the Sierras. I then stopped at Sierra City, where I was able to drop off my bear canister.

For the last few days, I’ve been walking through burn zones from a couple of massive fires that occurred in 2020 and 2021. There’s about 100 miles of burnt trail from these fires. Burn zones are quite unpleasant and also pose extra challenges.

Burnt trees are unstable, so you have to be selective about where you camp. One day, when I was ready to set up my tent, I discovered I was in the middle of a large stretch with no suitable campsites. I had to walk until about 10 pm before finding something suitable. I still have about 40 miles of this burn zone left, and I’m very much looking forward to getting out of it!

Wildfire season is well and truly underway this year - there are currently 6 closures on the PCT due to fires. Two of these were announced today, and three are on sections of trail I have not yet covered. The first closure I will face is due to the Shelly fire, near Etna, and another fire has just popped up near the California/Oregon border. There are no alternative routes, so I’ll likely have to skip from Mount Shasta to Ashland in Oregon, covering about 200 trail miles. It’s such a shame that wildfires are now so prevalent.

I have managed to increase my pace significantly since my last post. Although there is still a lot of elevation change, the trail is now smoother, allowing me to walk faster. It’s also at a lower altitude. I am now confident that I can finish before snow starts in Washington, especially given the miles I will likely have to skip!

posts.title: Days 84 - 90
posts.content:
I’m now in Mt Shasta City, off mile 1,502 (2,417). I’ve stopped here to resupply and buy new shoes, which I desperately need. I’ll try and get back to the trail this afternoon.

About a week ago, I crossed the divide between the Sierra Nevada and Cascade mountain ranges. The scenery has changed, and there are now a lot of volcanic features in the landscape. I’ve enjoyed the view of Mount Shasta over the last few days.

The heat has been the biggest challenge over the last week. One day, there was a high of 38 degrees. The volcanic rock seems to absorb and radiate the heat. I leave my tent open at night and sleep outside of my sleeping bag to try and stay cool.

In my last post, I had assumed I would be skipping the section of trail from here to Oregon. Luckily, progress has been made in controlling the Shelly fire, and the other fire near the border. I can now carry on to Etna, a town which had previously been evacuated. I’m quite happy about this. I’ll only have to skip about 60 miles - far less than the 200 miles I was anticipating.

posts.title: Days 91 - 98
posts.content:
I’m now in Ashland, which means that after three months, I have finally crossed the border into Oregon! Ashland is at mile 1719 (2766 km). I will stay in a hostel tonight, which I’m looking forward to, as it will be the first time I have slept in a bed in over a month!

The section between Mt Shasta and Etna was very nice. The first day had a massive climb of over 2000 m. I left early to avoid the heat, and spotted a bear in the distance, which is the first one I’ve seen.

After Etna, I had to skip some of the trail to get around the Shelley fire. Luckily, it’s almost fully contained now, and they are progressively opening the closed section. I was able to get a ride to a remote trailhead near the closure, meaning I only skipped 24 miles.

Recently, a large new fire started just behind me. Apparently it’s the fourth largest in California history. People I’ve talked to woke up one morning to thick smoke, with ash falling from the sky. Consequently, there is at least 100 miles worth of trail where people have had to skip ahead. I was very lucky to avoid this - I was only a few days ahead. There is now a very high concentration of hikers not far behind me. I will try to keep my pace up to stay in front.

There are still a lot of closures ahead due to fires. The next one is near Crater Lake, about 100 miles away. Luckily, the Crater Lake Rim Trail is still open, a detour which people usually take anyway. I’m very keen to see this section, so I hope it remains open.

posts.title: Days 99 - 107
posts.content:
I’m now in a town called Sisters. I got a ride here from Santiam Pass, which is at mile 2,002 (3,222 km). I am staying at a campground tonight and have a ride back to the trail early tomorrow morning.

After Ashland, I made my way to Mazama Village, which is next to Crater Lake. Fortunately, the Crater Lake Rim Trail was still open, which was quite spectacular. The trail was at a high elevation, which gave great views of the lake. I was also able to see the upcoming fires in the distance.

After the Rim Trail, I had to skip past a fire closure. This involved three different rides, with a brief stop in a town called Chemault. There was quite a lot of smoke, so I didn’t hang around for too long. I eventually made it to Shelter Cove, where I could get back on the PCT. I had to skip 68 miles due to this closure.

The scenery in Oregon has been very nice. The trail passes through a lot of forest, but there are also open meadows and lava fields. There are many volcanoes near the trail.

On Friday and Saturday, an event called PCT Days will be held in Cascade Locks at the Oregon/Washington border. I’m considering going to it as it would be a good opportunity to see people from the trail who I haven’t seen for a while. It’s too far for me to walk to, so I will have to figure out a ride if I am to make it in time.

posts.title: Days 108 - 115
posts.content:
I’ve arrived in Cascade Locks at the Oregon/Washington border. This is at mile 2,150 (3,460 km). The trail passes straight through the town, before going over the Bridge Of The Gods. This is a famous landmark on the PCT, which passes over the Columbia River into Washington.

The trail has been quite smooth from Sisters. There was still quite a lot of elevation change, but it was more gradual than in California. There have been great views of Mt Hood.

I made it to highway 35 on Friday morning, which allowed me to make the return trip into Cascade Locks for PCT Days. Hundreds of PCT hikers were packed into the small town, with camping available on an island in the middle of the river. Many vendors were set up for the event, so I took the opportunity to ask Thermarest for help fixing a leak in my air mattress. They found a defect covered under the warranty and gave me a brand new pad!

I made my way back to highway 35 on Sunday afternoon. From there, I walked the short distance to Timberline Lodge, another famous landmark of the PCT. I camped outside and went in for the breakfast buffet in the morning.

There has been a lot of uncertainty about Washington due to fire closures. Fires near the Canadian border have been burning close to the trail since early in the season. A couple of weeks ago, a new fire started near Mt Adams, about 90 miles north of here.

All of these fires are in logistically difficult locations. The closures are in remote areas, and it’s very difficult to find a ride around them. Consequently, people have been skipping massive sections of Washington. The closure near Mt Adams is only 22 miles, but people have been forced to skip about 150 miles.

This morning, my plan was to buy a bike and ride the rest of the way to Canada. However, my luck turned this afternoon when the PCTA released an official detour around the fire. It involves two days of road walking and a six day food carry. It’s worth it, as it will open up a massive section of the trail I thought I would miss.

I’m planning to slow down and enjoy more days off now that I’m ahead of schedule. By waiting a bit longer, I hope that parts of the trail further north will open up. This marks a shift in my approach - until now, I’ve been pushing hard to ensure I finish before the snow arrives. Tomorrow, I’ll rent a bike to explore the area and make the most of this time.

posts.title: Days 116 - 125
posts.content:
I’m now in Packwood, which is off White Pass at mile 2,298 (3,698 km). There happens to be a massive flea market here this week, which means there are hundreds of people from out of town. Luckily, there is a camping spot for PCT hikers, so I didn’t need to worry about accommodation.

From Cascade Locks, I had to take a detour around the Williams Mine Fire near Mt Adams. The closure of Trout Lake, the next resupply stop, was the biggest difficulty - it meant I needed to carry food for about 125 miles. It was a remote area with very little traffic, so I built in a big safety margin. My pack was very heavy, which made the first couple of days difficult. I had an abundance of food left at the end, so I was perhaps a bit too generous with the safety margin.

The detour took me off the PCT for a bit over a day. It involved a combination of forest roads and walking trails. I only saw one other PCT hiker in a 100 mile stretch.

There was a noticeable drop in temperature, and it rained for one day. There was a lot of dense forest, which made it difficult to dry things. I was very happy to have picked up an Ursak (a bear proof food bag) in Cascade Locks, as I was woken several times by rodents looking for food around my tent.

The detour rejoined the PCT before the Goat Rocks Wilderness. This area was a highlight of the PCT so far, with the best views since the Sierras. I scrambled up Old Snowy Mountain, which gave a great view of all three nearby volcanoes - Mt Rainier, Mt Adams and Mt St Helens. From there, the PCT descended down the Knife’s Edge - named for its path down the spine of a narrow ridge.

For the next 150 or so miles, I don’t have to worry about fires. There are still multiple closures ahead of that, but they are slowly being reopened. I hope that by the time I get there, the trail will either be open, or there will be alternatives available.

posts.title: Days 126 - 134
posts.content:
I’m now in Leavenworth, which is a Bavarian style village. I got a ride here yesterday from Stevens Pass at mile 2,467 (3,809 km). I shared a hotel room with some others last night and will head back to the trail this afternoon.

The gradient has definitely changed since Oregon. The trail seems to only ascend or descend, without flat sections in between. I’ve had to reduce my daily mileage expectations, as my pace is a lot slower, and the daylight hours are reducing.

There have been many berries on the side of the trail. I often see locals with containers collecting them. I’ve enjoyed being able to add some fresh food to my diet.

There is now a detour available around the fire in the next section. This means I can walk all the way to the border from here. People often say the Northern Cascades have some of the best scenery of the PCT, so I’m very happy this is available.

The detour is apparently quite challenging. It goes through rugged terrain which is not well maintained, and it has a lot of elevation gain. I’ll need to carry 6 days worth of food. I’ll join a couple of others for this section.

posts.title: Days 135 - 139
posts.content:
I’m now in Stehekin, off mile 2,575 (4,144 km) of the PCT. Stehekin is a remote lakeside town, and is only accessible by boat, floatplane or foot. It’s too expensive for a full resupply, so we’re just taking a quick stop here.

I’m lucky to be able to visit Stehekin. Less than a month ago, the town was evacuated due to the Pioneer Fire. The fire came within 100 feet of the buildings, and there were apparently 750 firefighters in town. In the photos below, you can see some of the burnt trees in the background, and a map showing how close the fire came to the town

After Stevens Pass, we took a detour to get around the closure due to the Miner’s Complex Fire. This took about three days. The trail was a lot steeper than the PCT, and overgrown at times. It went over three passes, and there was almost 5,000 m of elevation gain.

It was a shame to miss this section of the PCT. The PCT went past Mica Lake and through the Glacier Peak Wilderness, which are popular attractions. However, I did enjoy the detour. There were steep valleys, which had great scenery.

On the third day, we walked over a pass called Spider Gap. It was fun walking over the top of Spider Glacier (what’s left of it anyway). It was very cloudy, so the views weren’t perfect. This was a shame, because photos from clear days look amazing.

I’m now in the final stretch. Tomorrow, we’ll go into a town called Winthrop to resupply. There’s less than 100 miles left - we should be in Canada on Wednesday.

posts.title: Days 140 - 144
posts.content:
I made it to Canada! I reached the Northern Terminus two days ago, and carried on to Manning Park Resort. I stayed there for the night, and got a ride to Vancouver yesterday.

The last section was one of my favourites. There were great views of the Northern Cascades, which reminded me of the Sierra. On the last night, we stayed at a lake with a bear eating berries on the far side of it. On the last morning, we saw a Lynx, which I think are very rare to see.

I’ve got a couple of weeks before flying back to New Zealand. I’m currently in Whistler, where I’ll do a bit of mountain biking. If the PCT reopens in Oregon, I’ll consider going back to complete my missing miles. If not, I’ve done 96.5%… Close enough!

I’ve made a short video with one clip from almost every day on the PCT: https://youtu.be/eLqvFT9UGaI

And here are some stats from my trip:

Start date: 28th of April

Finish date: 18th of September

Days from start to finish: 144

Total distance walked: 4,269.71 km / 2,653.08 miles

Total elevation gain: 136,086 m / 446,476 feet

Average daily distance: 29.65 km / 18.42 miles

Average daily elevation gain: 945 m / 3,101 feet

Most distance in one day: 58.35 km / 36.26 miles

Biggest day: 53.19 km with 2,346 m elevation gain / 33.05 miles with 7,697 feet elevation gain

Rattlesnakes seen (or heard): 8

Bears seen: 4

Average start time: 08:12

Average finish time: 17:40

Miles skipped due to wildfires: 92.7 (149.2 km)

Miles detoured due to wildfires: 119.5 (192.3 km)

Zero days (days with no walking): 4

Nearo days (days walking significantly less than normal): 28

Resupplies: 27

Resupply boxes sent: 1

Pairs of shoes: 5
